title: STANDARD FOR BLACK, WHITE AND GREEN PEPPERS CXS 326-2017

sections.section: STANDARD FOR BLACK, WHITE AND GREEN PEPPERS CXS 326-2017
sections.summary: Document title for the Codex Standard for Black, White and Green Peppers (CXS 326‑2017), noting adoption in 2017 and amendments in 2021 and 2022.

sections.section: 2022 Amendment The following amendments were made to the text of the standard following decisions taken at the forty-fifth session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.
sections.summary: Notifies a 2022 amendment that revised the labelling provision for non‑retail containers (Section 8.3) to replace the prior detailed clause with a requirement to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Container of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).
Scope: applies to black, white and green peppers offered for direct consumption, use as a food processing ingredient, or for repackaging, and excludes peppers intended for industrial processing.

Section heading marked NA followed by Table 1B listing basic physical characteristics of BWG (black, white, green) peppers, giving approximate whole-berry diameters and descriptions of typical shape and pericarp surface for each color.

Three classes/grades (Class/Grade I, II and III) apply to BWG peppers and Table 2 sets class- and colour-specific physical quality limits (e.g., bulk density minima for black/white, maximum percentages for light berries, extraneous vegetable matter, foreign matter, black berries, broken berries, mouldy berries, insect-defiled berries, mammalian/other excreta in mg/kg, and pinheads for black pepper) for whole berries.

Table 3 specifies chemical requirements for whole BWG peppers by class and colour (maximum moisture, total ash, acid‑insoluble ash; minimum non‑volatile ether extract, volatile oils and piperine where applicable), and Table 4 specifies chemical requirements for ground black and white pepper (moisture, total ash, ether extract, volatile oil, crude fibre, piperine, acid‑insoluble ash), noting ground peppers include forms per Section 2.2(b).
A container sample that fails to meet one or more applicable quality requirements in Section 3.2 (excluding those based on sample averages) is to be considered defective.

A lot meets the quality requirements of Section 3.2 when the number of defectives (per Section 3.3) does not exceed the acceptance number c of the applicable sampling plan; for attributes judged by sample averages, a lot is acceptable if the average meets tolerances and no individual sample is excessively out of tolerance.

Preservatives listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) for food category 12.2.1 (herbs and spices) are permitted only in green peppers that conform to this standard.

Products covered by this standard must comply with the maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

It is recommended that products be prepared and handled in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for Low Moisture Foods (CXC 75-2015, Annex III) and other relevant Codex texts, and that they comply with any microbiological criteria established per the Principles and Guidelines for Microbiological Criteria (CXG 21-1997).
Containers shall be filled as fully as practicable without impairing quality and consistent with a proper declaration of contents.

Products covered by this standard must be labelled in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985), with additional specific provisions set out in the following subsections.

The product name shall be 'black pepper' (pepper corn), 'white pepper' or 'green pepper'; the nature may indicate style and grade (per Section 2.2), country of origin is optional, commercial identification should include class/grade and optionally variety, and an inspection mark is optional.
Labelling of non‑retail containers must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers in Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Compliance with this standard is to be checked using the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) that are relevant to the standard's provisions.
Bibliographic notes listing referenced Codex standards and guidance documents cited in the standard (non‑substantive reference material).
